data.title: No Flurry Of Optimism: Why I'm Downgrading Douglas Dynamics
data.description:
Nearly a year ago, I decided to revisit my bullish thesis on Douglas Dynamics (NYSE:PLOW). As the company’s ticker symbol hints at, the business is engaged in the production and upfitting of commercial work truck attachments and equipment, with the firm's emphasis being on not only the snowplow industry, but also on sand and salt spreaders for light trucks. In my previous article about the business, I mentioned how, despite a decline in revenue, the overall financial performance of the business was improving on a year over year basis. Management was forecasting continued growth for the rest of the 2024 fiscal year and shares were priced at levels that I considered to be attractive.

Unfortunately, things have not gone exactly according to plan since then. Financial performance continues to be mixed and the stock has plummeted by 24.1% at a time when the S&P 500 is down 1.3%. Although shares have gotten cheaper on an EV to EBITDA basis since then, they have gotten more expensive relative to adjusted operating cash flow. This, combined with broader economic uncertainty, has me feeling a bit more cautious than I was previously. We are seeing the firm continuing to transform its operations into something that, hopefully, will create more value for shareholders as time goes on. But for now, I think the prudent approach is to downgrade the company to a ‘hold’.

Time for a downgrade

Given the time that has passed since we last looked at Douglas Dynamics, I think it would be helpful to cover, briefly, what the company is and how it operates. As I mentioned at the start of this article, the firm operates as a producer and upfitter of commercial work truck attachments and equipment that is dedicated to the snowplow industry, as well as to the sand and salt spreader categories for light trucks. Examples of its work can be seen in the image below.

This is an interesting space to me. It's also a business model that cannot be applicable everywhere. In the image below, you can see a map of where the business operates, with the blue areas representing its core markets, the green area representing expansion markets, and the gray area representing markets the business currently has no intention of playing in. The gray area really fits into two different categories. The first would be those, like the western parts of Canada and states such as Montana, Idaho, and Wyoming, that have low population density. The second would be states that the business just hasn't decided to allocate its resources to or that don't have large enough markets for it to bother with.

Primarily, Douglas Dynamics has been focused on providing line of snowplows and sand and salt spreaders for light trucks, as well as related parts and accessories, for its customers. These operations fall under its Work Truck Attachments segment. Even though the business has made sure to expand its offerings over the past few years, it is worth noting that 82% of the revenue generated by this segment involve the sale of snow and ice control equipment.

In the image above, you can see that, even as snowfall has been declining since around 2014, the company has succeeded in slowly growing its revenue over time. This is certainly encouraging and seems counterintuitive. But price increases, organic expansion, and other methods, have allowed it to grow sales nonetheless. The reason why I said that sales have historically involved the Work Truck Attachment segment is because, while that was the case, it is no longer the case. In fact, in 2024, it became the second-largest operating segment, generating sales of $256 million compared to the $312.5 million generated by the Work Truck Solutions segment. These figures compared to the $382.3 million and the $233.8 million, respectively, generated just two years earlier.

The Work Truck Solutions segment for the company produces municipal snow and ice control products and also provides a complementary line of upfitting services and products for its customers. Examples of products here include truck and vehicle upfits that the company attaches, truck bodies, racking, and storage solutions. This part of the business is focused in large part on up fitting Class 3 to Class 8 trucks and other commercial work vehicles. On top of this, the company also provides the roughly 2,700 customers that it services with specialized storage solutions that can be used for trucks and vans, as well as cable pulling equipment that can be used for trucks.

Over the last three fiscal years now, Douglas Dynamics has seen its revenue fall from $616.1 million to $568.5 million. The pain point, as I already mentioned, involved the Work Truck Attachments segment, with revenue contracting sharply from $382.3 million to $256 million. Even though the company has been able to increase its prices for this segment, overall volumes have dropped enough to bring revenue down. Management claimed that this was because of low snowfall in its core markets. But the real surprise has been the increase associated with its Work Truck Solutions segment. From 2022 to 2023, revenue jumped from $233.8 million to $276.5 million.

Management claimed that this was because of higher volumes caused by improved chassis availability after experiencing a couple of years of supply chain constraints caused by the COVID-19 pandemic. Higher prices and increased sales of the company’s own purchased chassis that it typically sells at cost, also contributed to this growth. From 2023 to 2024, revenue grew further from $276.5 million to $312.5 million. Management claimed that higher volumes on improved throughput, higher prices, and higher sales of its company-purchased chassis can be thanked for this growth.

Even as revenue dropped, net profits for the company improved, jumping from $38.6 million to $56.2 million. However, the gain achieved in 2024 was a bit deceptive because the business booked a $42.3 million gain on a sale leaseback transaction. This involved the sale of seven properties that the company previously owned, with three located in Milwaukee, Wisconsin, and the others split between Iowa, Illinois, Maine, and Michigan. These properties comprise about 780,000 square feet and involve both manufacturing and upfitting space. The business received $64.2 million from this sale. However, it now has long-term lease payments that it has to pay in order to use those properties.

On an adjusted basis, we saw net profits actually fall from $43.5 million to $35.2 million during this window of time. Other profitability metrics have been all over the map. Operating cash flow, for instance, inched up from $40 million to $41.1 million. But the other profitability metrics have fallen. If we adjust for changes in working capital, for instance, we get a decline in operating cash flow from $62.5 million to $42.6 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the business fell from $86.8 million to $79.3 million.

Even though we have had the bright spot that involves the company’s Work Truck Solutions business, it is important to note that margins are still lower for this piece of the pie than they are for the Work Truck Attachments business. Admittedly, they have improved in recent years, climbing from 3.67% of sales to 9.89%. This is an encouraging trend and could be a sign of a future powerhouse for the business. But these margins still fall quite a bit short from the 18.93% that the Work Truck Attachments segment's all last year.

Even though revenue for the company has fallen in recent years, management did announce in February that they expect sales for 2025 to come in higher at between $610 million and $650 million. The midpoint of guidance there would imply a year-over-year growth rate of 10.8%. This is supported by a backlog of $348 million, compared to the $296.3 million reported one year earlier. However, management did specify that this assumes that the economy remains stable and that supply chain issues don't pose a problem. Given the tariff situation, which risks throwing the US into a nasty recession highlighted by stagflation, I think that we will eventually see a downward revision in these forecasts.

On the bottom line, management said that adjusted earnings per share should range between $1.30 and $2.10. This is a massive range that gives us a midpoint estimate of $40.1 million for adjusted net income. That's up slightly from the $35.2 million reported for last year. Management also anticipates EBITDA of between $75 million and $95 million. The midpoint here would likely translate to adjusted operating cash flow of $45.7 million.

In the chart above, you can see how shares of the company are currently priced using these estimates and using historical results for the 2024 fiscal year. The stock is not expensive. In fact, it looks cheap on an EV to EBITDA basis and looks to be around the low end of the fair value range when it comes to the other two profitability metrics. Relative to similar companies, shares might be a bit on the pricey side of things. In the table below, you can see the firm stacked up against five similar entities. On both a price to earnings basis and an EV to EBITDA basis, you can see that three of the five companies are cheaper than Douglas Dynamics is. This number rises to four of the five when using the price to operating cash flow scenario.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Douglas Dynamics 14.8 12.2 8.3
Trinity Industries (TRN) 7.5 28.1 5.1
Mueller Industries (MLR) 5.9 3.7 4.4
Wabash National (WNC) 65.4 9.1 15.0
The Shyft Group (SHYF) 7.0 7.2 7.7
Terex Corporation (TEX) 15.4 3.7 9.9

There are some concerns that I have about the company that have also gone into my decision to downgrade it from a ‘buy’ to a ‘hold’. For starters, the tariff situation poses considerable risk for any business that involves imports and high expenditures. The machinery and equipment space should be particularly impacted, though we don't yet know exactly to what extent this will be the case. We can get an idea, though, of the challenges facing the company by looking at steel prices.

In the company's annual report, it mentions steel 56 times. Rising prices cause its overall costs to rise and can create the risk of decreased profit margins. Specifically, steel presents the highest raw material and component costs for the business. In the chart above, you can see pricing for the last three years associated with US Midwest Domestic Hot-Rolled Coil Steel futures contracts. This is the exact type of steel that the company acquires hedges for in order to protect itself from significant price fluctuations. However, business only has a little under half of its expected steel consumption hedged, and only for the window from August 1st of this year through the end of the year. Under its hedging agreement, it will make fixed payments of $819 per short ton, irrespective of what steel prices are at. But as the aforementioned chart illustrates, from January 20th of this year to the present day, steel prices have shot up from $778 to $938. Tariffs risk increasing that further in the near term.

Another risk involves climate change. In the US, from 1970 to 2023, annual snowfall dropped by 64%. This is part of the problem when discussing access to fresh water, as evidenced by the fact that, since 1955, snow derived fresh water in the western US has fallen by between 15% and 30%. For the Northern Hemisphere as a whole, it's expected that snow cover will fall another 15% by the year 2100. None of this bodes well for a company that generates most of its revenue from snow.

Takeaway

At this time, I do still think that Douglas Dynamics is an interesting business. Management has done a fine job considering the challenges that the company has faced. But at the end of the day, I no longer view this as a strong enough prospect to justify a bullish rating. Short-term and long-term issues, combined with the change in the valuation of the company, justify downgrading it to a ‘hold’ in my book.

data.title: 89bio: A Buy With Strong Market Opportunity In MASH And SHTG
data.description:
Introduction

We initiate a Buy rating for 89bio, Inc. (NASDAQ:ETNB), which, in our view, presents a good long-term opportunity, mainly because of its lead candidate, pegozafermin. This drug might be able to help in two challenging indications with few options right now: metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH, formerly NASH) and severe hypertriglyceridemia (SHTG).

We think the data so far from Phase 2 studies look encouraging. If the Phase 3 trials go well, we believe there could be real upside. Right now, the stock trades around $6, and we feel that reflects hesitation in the market that could shift with strong data.

Stock Performance

Over the last year, 89bio’s stock has been somewhat volatile. Shares climbed past $10 earlier this year, but dropped under $6 more recently. Year to date, the stock has dropped over 25%. The company has a current market capitalization around $883 million.

Pipeline Developments

Pegozafermin in MASH

The company’s MASH program is focused on pegozafermin, a lab-made version of FGF21. The idea is to slow or even reverse liver damage. In the ENLIVEN Phase 2b trial, around 26% to 27% of people on the higher dose showed at least a one-stage drop in fibrosis without things getting worse otherwise in just 24 weeks. Only 7% of the placebo group saw that kind of change. And that benefit seemed to stick around through 48 weeks.

The next big moments will be the Phase 3 ENLIGHTEN-Fibrosis trial, which should have topline data released in the first half of 2027, and the ENLIGHTEN-Cirrhosis trial in 2028. We see these readouts as likely key for figuring out whether this drug can move forward toward approval. Specifically, we think that, if the company can build upon the results from the Phase 2b trial by demonstrating similar efficacy levels -- which, as we will discuss, are comparable, if not superior to the newly approved MASH drug, Rezdiffra -- while maintaining its solid safety profile, the FDA will likely approve the drug.

Pegozafermin in SHTG

For SHTG, pegozafermin targets people with triglyceride levels above 500 mg/dL. These patients are more likely to face pancreatitis and heart problems. In the ENTRIGUE Phase 2 study, the drug lowered triglycerides by about 63% in eight weeks, and this was true even for patients already on other medications. It also helped with non-HDL cholesterol and apolipoprotein B.

The ENTRUST Phase 3 trial has wrapped up enrollment, and results are expected in early 2026. We think those findings will be important for confirming if the benefits hold up in a bigger group over a longer trial duration.

Competing Drug Overview

MASH

Rezdiffra vs. Pegozafermin

Rezdiffra, from Madrigal Pharmaceuticals (MDGL), was the first drug approved by the FDA just for MASH. It works by targeting thyroid hormone receptor beta and showed a 24% improvement in fibrosis over a full year. But, based on available data we reviewed, it appears that benefit seems to build slowly.

Pegozafermin, on the other hand, had similar results after just 24 weeks in its Phase 2b study, which we see as a key point of differentiation. In our opinion, Rezdiffra might make sense for patients with milder fibrosis or for those who need a pill rather than a shot. But pegozafermin might find its niche with patients who have more advanced disease and need quicker results.

Efruxifermin vs. Pegozafermin

We see Efruxifermin from Akero Therapeutics (AKRO) is another serious contender. In the Phase 2b HARMONY trial, 75% of patients on the 50 mg dose had a one-stage fibrosis improvement by Week 96, and 36% saw a two-stage improvement — far more than the placebo group. The placebo-adjusted gain in fibrosis response grew from about 21% at Week 24 to 52% at Week 96. In patients with F3 fibrosis, 68% got at least a one-stage improvement with treatment, while just 14% did on placebo.

We see this as a very strong result. Still, it took nearly two years to see it. Pegozafermin saw 26% to 27% improvement after only 24 weeks. A 2024 review of FGF21 drugs indicated pegozafermin led the way for at least one-stage fibrosis improvement without MASH worsening, while efruxifermin was better at hitting two-stage improvements on NAS scores. So, our takeaway here is that pegozafermin may work faster and have a stronger safety record, while efruxifermin may have more efficacy if used longer.

Nevertheless, a drug's safety profile is not only vitally important to regulators, but also to treating physicians and patients. Efruxifermin’s trial saw more side effects — mostly around injection sites and digestion. Risk of things like diarrhea and nausea was clearly higher, whereas Pegozafermin seemed better tolerated, with fewer side effects overall. Thus, with respect to this 1:1 matchup, we see safety as a critical point of differentiation, as Pegozafermin may be the preferred choice for patients who need to stay on treatment long-term.

Other Drugs vs. Pegozafermin

The list of other competing drugs is extensive. While we do not intend to analyze every single one in this article, we will briefly note that others include semaglutide and tirzepatide from Novo Nordisk (NVO) and Eli Lilly (LLY), respectively, which work well for fat reduction and have fibrosis benefits. Survodutide from Boehringer Ingelheim, a GLP-1/glucagon mix, also has shown promise. But many of these have side effects that, in our view, patients may not always be able to tolerate. Pegozafermin’s balance of safety and speed may help it stand out.

SHTG

In SHTG, Ionis (IONS) and AstraZeneca’s (AZN) olezarsen cuts triglycerides by 70% to 80%. That is more than pegozafermin (63% reduction). But this stronger drop may come with trade-offs.

Based on recent trial data, olezarsen’s injections have been linked to more site reactions and patients often need more blood tests. There are also added concerns about long-term monitoring.

In our view, this could cause doctors and patients to consider alternative treatments with less adverse effects and monitoring requirements. As noted above, Pegozafermin has shown a solid 63% reduction, and it might also be easier to tolerate. We think that could tip the scales in its favor, assuming, of course, Pegozafermin gets approved and successfully commercialized.

Market Opportunity

The market for MASH drugs appears to be very large. By 2033, it is expected that the market will hit $31.76 billion. Rezdiffra’s sales expectations of up to $8 billion in peak annual sales show the size of the opportunity. Still, its slow effect might make it less appealing for people who need faster results. If pegozafermin’s Phase 3 trials go well, it could grab a meaningful share of that market, assuming it gets approved and successfully commercialized.

In SHTG, the opportunity is much smaller, as the market is expected to hit only $3.61 billion by 2035. That being said, we believe pegozafermin, with its good safety and solid performance in trials, could acquire a significant share of this market, assuming it gets approved and successfully commercialized.

Financial Overview

Regarding the company's financial position, quarterly losses increased to $118.4 million in Q4 2024. The company had $440 million in cash at year-end. A new equity raise in Q1 2025 pushed that to around $727 million. Our review of cash flow from operations indicates a burn rate of $121.6 million in Q4 2024.

Based on the current cash position, we estimate a runway into mid to late 2026, which could obviously change with any unexpected pipeline developments (e.g., clinical advancements, new drug introductions, clinical delays, etc.). We think investors can expect the company to return to the capital markets around that timeframe, which could have a dilutive effect on shareholders.

Risks

We see multiple risks for investors. In particular, clinical trials involving pegozafermin might not hit their endpoints, which could result in delays and/or program termination.

A failure in the MASH clinical trial involving pegozafermin could drastically lower the market opportunity for the company, as it could force to it put all its eggs in the SHTG basket, which is a much smaller market than MASH. Regardless, if either program fails, it could lower confidence in the other program, and thereby put downward pressure on the stock.

There are also serious commercial risks. As alluded to above, the MASH market is very competitive. Our bullish thesis is predicated on pegozafermin carving out a significant market share based on its potentially strong efficacy and safety profile. Of course, our prediction could prove completely wrong, in which case, 89bio would potentially have to fall back on the drug's sales in SHTG, which, again, is a much smaller market.

Finally, as we noted in the financial overview, costs might rise, which could force the company to return to the capital market and further dilute shareholders.

Strategic Alternatives

We believe the Phase 3 data from the ENTRUST trial in SHTG, expected in Q1 2026, will be closely watched. Also important will be the results from the ENLIGHTEN-Fibrosis and ENLIGHTEN-Cirrhosis trials in 2027 and 2028. These may drive new investor interest. If the data are strong, we think the company could attract partners or even buyout interest.

Conclusion

In our view, 89bio has a good shot at carving out a place in two major markets. Pegozafermin has shown encouraging early results in both MASH and SHTG. If Phase 3 data lines up with what we have seen so far, we think the drug could become a major player.

We think the current valuation may not reflect this potential. For long-term investors, we believe the stock offers real upside. For these reasons, we initiate a Buy rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Bank of America: Wall Street Seems Afraid To Tell It Like It Is (Sell Rating)
data.description:
I'll briefly review Bank of America's (NYSE:BAC) quarterly results announced earlier this week. But first, I am going to pause and ask anyone reading this to think about something: what does a "rating" on a stock mean? Is it worth the digital paper it is printed on? I'm talking specifically about Wall Street analysts, since they earn a fine wage for doing that narrow job, within a sector or industry.

Because this chart shows the fallacy, and the downright silliness, of the Wall Street ratings game. Emphasis on "game," since I suspect there are sociology studies going back decades that explain how investors are influenced by hearing a stock is a "buy." I started at a couple of big brokerage firms before entering investment management in a fiduciary capacity. I've retired from that, but I recall what I used to tell clients: "I spent the first 12 years of my career inside of big Wall Street, and I'm spending the rest of my career telling people what they can and can't believe from it." So far, so good.

BAC has been rated buy, on average, continuously since late 2019. In particular, the last 3 years have not produced even a little budge in confidence toward the stock by this group of "experts." I guess a lot of firms are in the running for investment banking business with BAC! At least, that was the old rationale for not rating a stock other than a buy, unless it was in deep trouble and everyone knew it. In which case, it would merit a "hold" rating!

BAC has produced a total return of 8% in the past 3 years. In large part because it fell 30%, and spent the rest of the time catching up to break even. This is not unlike the average stock in the S&P 500 since that time.

Going back further, to just after the start of the ratings history above, BAC has gained 21 since the start of 2020. That's more than 5 years. So if nothing else, it has been consistent, with low single-digit annualized returns resulting from a classic stock pattern in this post-pandemic era: the rallies are remembered, but the prior declines forgotten.

With that backdrop, and a stock market looking for leadership, I don't expect to find it here.

Bank of America Q1 Earnings Were Solid

Bank of America reported good Q1 earnings. EPS came in at $0.90 while expectations were $0.82.

These good earnings were mainly the result of positive KPIs. Organic growth struck me positively, with the bank succeeding on several fronts. As the image below shows, in Q1 the bank grew new accounts in consumer banking, relationships in wealth management, a higher volume of credit cards, along with several records such as record equities sales, benefiting from the volatility of the current scenario.

Ironically, while rumors of hedge fund blowups abound, BAC is showing that trading desks can still profit.

The stability of the provisions together with the confirmation of the outlook for 2025 reinforce Bank of America's resilience. But that's the business, not the stock.

Even in a scenario that seems chaotic, the bank is doing very well. In the chart below, for instance, you can see that even taking into account -$450 million of interest rate cuts over the year, the bank still manages to add around a billion to net interest income, which in the full year translates into a 6%-7% growth in NII, a reasonable growth for a solid company at this level.

Using the same words mentioned in the earnings call, this quarter of 2025 was a “solid start to 2025” for Bank of America, and consequently for the shareholders as well.

BAC Looks Like A Solid Company Right Now

The main indicators I like to use to analyze banks are price-to-book and ROE.

Bank of America's ROE, at first glance, may seem less attractive, delivering something close to 10% in Q1 and almost 14% when analyzing ROTCE. Meanwhile, in the last 12 months JPMorgan (JPM) delivered over 17%, and Wells Fargo (WFC) delivered just over 11%.

But the good thing is that, adjusted for valuation, BAC looks quite reasonable. Its price-to-book is only 1x, which for an ROE above double-digit is already interesting for the shareholder and is capable of producing sustainable returns, especially given the bank's solidity.

The forward price-to-earnings, as shown in the chart below, is also the most interesting when compared to its two main peers listed.

In short, BAC's Q1 earnings were solid, and although in parts they were in line with expectations, in the current chaotic market, just the reinforcement that the institution is capable of producing good returns and continues to have a stable outlook for 2025 is enough to say that it was a good quarter.

But about that stock...

This is where I draw the line, nearly every day, with every stock. I am not the typical investor we see in 2025 that sees every dip as a buying opportunity. Any dip COULD be one, but the odds are stacked high against it. I looked at several other large banks, and while there are moments (usually earnings report-related) that treat bank stocks individually, during markets like this, they tend to rise and fall together.

Stock picking is hard enough without the additional impediment of "indexation" where ETFs have become so big, that stocks of a feather flock together. BAC is a fine company, and certainly a success story post-global financial crisis (though as we all know, they had a little help avoiding disaster). But when it comes to putting a priority on owning a stock like this, I'm just not there currently. Not in this market.

The technical chart confirms for me that this is yet another high risk/modest reward "opportunity." So I'm not buying the dip here. Because after years of netting out to very low positive returns, if this stock is somehow preparing to make a giant up move, anywhere near or through its all-time high (30% up from Thursday's close), my guess is there will either be time to get in, or it will flame out quickly, as it did with the rest of the market a couple of weeks ago.

There's just nothing to see here right now, other than a great, well-run bank whose stock price continues to be unable to produce returns worth the risk.

data.title: MTR Corporation: Watch Rising Leverage And Revenue Stability
data.description:
My view of MTR Corporation Limited (OTCPK:MTCPY) (OTCPK:MTRJF) (0066.HK) or "MCL" remains Neutral. The prior August 19, 2024 update evaluated its bottom-line and cash distributions.

I'm worried about MCL's credit health, which might get worse. But the company's predictable revenue streams provide downside protection. This justifies a Hold rating for the stock.

Recent Funding For MCL Sends A Warning About Credit Risks

MCL disclosed a new $3B bond issuance at the start of April. Its financial burden could become heavier over time.

The company's net gearing had already increased by +5 ppts ("percentage points") in the past year to 32% as of December 31, 2024. I estimate that this metric will go up further to 44%, taking into account the latest financing.

Its earnings will also be negatively affected by the higher amount of debt.

The borrowing cost for the additional funds raised is 4.8%. This is 110 bps ("basis points") above MCL's actual FY24 interest rate.

My projections suggest that annual finance expenses for the company will rise by HK$1 billion, or about 6% of its '24 normalized net profit.

The stock's balance sheet is likely to weaken going forward, considering its investing plan presented below.

MCL's Expected Capital Outlay For The Next 3 Years

MCL's yearly capex ("capital expenditures") are anticipated to grow by +56% to over HKD 30B in the 2025-2027 time frame.

Its average FY23-24 operating cashflow was HK$14,800M, or half of the capital investments required. The company secured HKD 23 billion ("$3B") of fresh financing as mentioned earlier, but that's less than a single year's capex.

It's inevitable that MCL will have to borrow more monies. I forecast that its debt ("after deducting cash") will roughly double between 2024 and 2027. This will bring the company's net leverage to as high as 53% by end-FY27.

The last time MCL's gearing surpassed the 50 percent mark was two decades ago in 2003.

The stock's valuation re-rating potential is limited in view of its unfavorable credit outlook. This is why I'm not rating the name as a Buy.

But MCL's Topline Will Be Resilient In The Near-Term

MCL is a defensive shelter for investors, so it doesn't deserve to be a Sell.

The company's geographical and business mix gives me confidence that it can register stable revenue for the foreseeable future.

MCL doesn't operate in or export to the United States. It isn't directly exposed to any potential or actual tariffs imposed by the Trump administration.

Assuming a full-blown trade war between multiple nations happens, there is less to be concerned in MCL's case. The company generated the majority, or 62% of its top line from domestic ("Hong Kong and Mainland China") operations. Australia and Europe contributed its remaining sales for the recent year.

Separately, MCL runs railway and residential property development businesses offering steady income.

The number of passengers for MCL's Hong Kong local rail operations declined modestly by -2% in the first two months of 2025. As a comparison, the same metric saw a +1% rise for full-year 2024.

Demand for the company's public transport services is fairly inelastic, as people need to commute on a day-to-day basis. The financial contribution from this business shouldn't fluctuate substantially from year to year.

Also, MCL noted at HSBC Holdings' (HSBC) latest investor event that its residential real estate unit has a high degree of visibility. It has yet to recognize revenue from approximately 11,000 sold units, which are scheduled to be constructed in the subsequent two years. I sourced the management's comments from a March 31 HSBC report ("not available publicly") with the title "Global Investment Summit 2025 Key Takeaways".

MCL's property division reported a FY2024 operating profit of HK$10B from almost 3,000 units. Given its huge backlog, it's reasonable to think that this segment can deliver at least similar earnings in FY25-26.

The consensus financial forecasts taken from S&P Capital IQ imply that MCL's topline and net income will be largely flattish for this year and 2026. I think this is a fair assumption.

I don't see MCL raising dividends, as its leverage will most probably rise in the coming years, as mentioned above.

But the stock should be able to sustain its current per-share distribution of HKD 1.31. This translates into a 5% dividend yield and adds to its "safe haven stock" appeal. As I have detailed in this section, its revenues for the short term are stable.

My Closing Thoughts

I still have a mixed opinion of MCL, making it a Hold-rated name.

This stock could be an ideal candidate for risk-averse investors seeking refuge from the economic uncertainty and trade turbulence. It boasts a mid-single-digit yield supported by topline resilience.

On the flip side, MCL's capital budget has expanded in a big way, and that necessitates more debt financing. As the company's gearing goes up in due course, it becomes a less attractive investment proposition from the credit risk angle.

The stock is also at a reasonable valuation using a yield-based analysis.

In the last five years, MTR traded ("on average") at a +0.4 ppts premium over the 10-year US Treasury, which is 4.3% at the time of writing. This equates to a target distribution yield of 4.7%, which isn't that far from its current dividend-to-price ratio of 5.0%.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Huntington Ingalls Industries: Limited Upside For U.S. Navy Shipbuilder
data.description:
In February, I covered Huntington Ingalls Industries (NYSE:HII) with a $210 price target based on FY2026 earnings. The current stock price is $220, indicating a 30% increase in the stock price and sharply outperforming the broader market, which lost 11.9%. In this report, I will discuss why the stock price has sharply increased, and I will discuss whether the stock has any additional upside or whether it is best to take profits.

Why Did Huntington Ingalls Industries Stock Surge?

Before we address why the stock price has sharply increased, it is important to highlight why the stock price has declined in the first place. In October 2024, HII stock declined sharply following the removal of the 5-year free cash flow outlook. In February, the stock price plunged once again after the company reported weaker than expected results.

So, the major reason for the stock price declines has been weak earnings and outlooks and that can be attributed to the challenges that shipbuilders face in the supply chain and more prominently in the supply chain for submarines. That is a complex supply chain that had to be restarted after the pandemic while inflation increased costs in the supply chain base and US shipbuilding expertise has also been dwindling.

Cuts to the defense budgets might also not have helped investor sentiment, and the notion by the Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth that US aircraft carriers are vulnerable may also not have helped.

It is indeed true that aircraft carriers are vulnerable in the current environment due to the fielding of hypersonics by adversaries. However, this does not render aircraft carriers obsolete and simply means that the US has to beef up its missile tracking and missile defense capabilities.

Furthermore, the US is looking to increase defense budgets to 5% of GDP instead of decreasing the budget, and President Trump signed a shipbuilding order. It is not the case that this is a U-turn on demand for US Navy ships or a realization that things need to change. The US Navy and the commercial industrial base have already highlighted the need to grow capacity and skill within the industrial base to deliver on the US Navy’s procurement plans. President Trump has considered looking for foreign ship builders to fulfill part of the demand, but generally parties seem to be aligned that the industrial base needs to expand and become more efficient and more skilled.

While things will not change overnight, we see firm support for naval vessels. President Trump wants a bigger US Navy and that is a positive, given that the way things currently are going the US Navy fleet will decline in size. What is missing at this point is a tangible plan with progress parameters. However, overall, the fear that investments for procurement and into the industrial base may stall are tapering.

Expectations for Huntington Ingalls Industries In Q1 2025

Huntington Ingalls Industries will report first quarter earnings on the 1st of May. Analysts are expecting the company to report $2.79 billion in sales and $2.85 in core earnings per share. The stock price has tumbled following the prior two earnings releases, but the company does not have an extremely bad track record on its earnings surprise.

The company missed expectations on revenues and EPS three out of the past 12 quarters, including the two prior quarters. So, there is some risk, but it is not the case that there is a track record of missing estimates. The reality, however, is that with supply chain disruptions and inflation, the company is more prone to cost overruns and delays.

Little Upside Left For Huntington Ingalls Industries

With the significant increase in stock prices, it is important to assess whether there is any upside left for the stock.

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Huntington Ingalls Industries has reached and exceeded my price target of $210.33 for FY2026 that I established in my prior report. So, any additional upside has to come from a lift in earnings estimates or free cash flow or an expansion of the trading multiple, and we see that this is not the case.

EBITDA is set to grow 10.6% annually, but the EBITDA estimates for the years 2025-2027 have been lowered by 2% and by 6% on free cash flow. That brings us to a $202.78 price target for FY2026, which has already been reached. So, for near term investors I would say that the stock is a sell and for long-term investors, HII is a better hold than buy at this point.

Conclusion: Upside Has Materialized

The stock price has sharply increased and is now trading 8% higher than my new $202.78 price target. So, there is no compelling investment case for HII at this point and as a result, I am marking the stock a hold. There is a sense of urgency to increase shipbuilding capacity in the US, but that will not materialize overnight, while the earnings estimates have come down. So, the compelling upside I previously saw is not there anymore as the stock price did increase as expected and the earnings expectations tapers in the meantime.

data.title: ARDC: We're Back In This Multi-Sector Credit CEF
data.description:
Recent market volatility has opened up a number of new income opportunities. One of these is the Ares Dynamic Credit Allocation Fund (NYSE:ARDC) - a multi-sector credit CEF. The fund has sold off sharply, and its discount opened up to a double-digit level before rallying slightly. The fund is down 18% from its 1-year peak; it trades at a current yield of 10.6% and an 8.4% discount.

We used to carry a significant allocation to ARDC. However, in response to stretched valuations in terms of both underlying assets and a tight discount, we exited the position in early 2024 into a combination of mostly baby bonds as well as BDCs.

The idea was to move into a more resilient allocation in case markets hit a bump in the road. The bump is finally here, and we are starting to step back into the fund at a much more attractive valuation. Moreover, because the position that ARDC was rotated into has held up quite a bit better, there is, in effect, more capital in the portfolio to put to use than had the ARDC position been kept to date.

Below we highlight the fund's portfolio, income profile and competitive advantages. We would look to add to our position if credit spreads widen past 5.5% and/or the discount moves wider of 15%.

Fund Snapshot

ARDC is a combination of loans (39% of the overall portfolio), corporate bonds (32% of the overall portfolio) and CLO securities. The CLO sleeve is composed of both CLO Equity (13.4% of the overall portfolio) and CLO Debt (17.5% of the overall portfolio).

It's odd the fund doesn't break down its CLO sector allocation. Outside of CLOs, its sector overweights are Energy and Technology.

The fund carries little in the form of CCC-rated securities. Its 14% unrated allocation is largely its CLO Equity holdings.

Income Profile

There are some conflicting signals about the fund's income. Monthly net investment income over 2024 was $0.1125 which is on target relative to its $0.1125 distribution rate. However, if we strip out the 2024 first half net investment income sourced from the semi-annual report, we get net investment income of $0.0967. This is a bigger drop than we would expect from the pass-through of Fed rate cuts last year, so there could be some noise in the numbers.

There are also two supporting factors. For one, the fund's monthly Section 19a releases have not shown any part of the distributions sourced from anything other than investment income. This suggests its distribution coverage is north of 100%.

And two, if we do a back-of-the-envelope calculation of the fund's internal yield on price, we get a yield that is higher than its distribution rate.

The fund's floating rate assets of around $360m exceed its floating-rate liabilities of around $106m. The net floating-rate position is around 72% of the NAV. This is why its distributions increased in 2022 and decreased most recently. If the Fed continues to push rates lower, we cannot rule out further cuts to the distribution.

However, there are several mitigants to this.

One, the fund's floating-rate position is less than that of a loan CEF. Only 70% of the drop in short-term rates gets passed through to net investment income.

Two, the 3-month / 5-year yield curve (i.e. the one that captures the relationship between floating-rate assets like loans and high-yield corporate bonds) has inverted in this latest market sell-off. Not only that, but its current level is unusually low. Over the last couple of decades, only recently was it more inverted. It will take a couple of Fed rate cuts for the base rate behind floating-rate assets to move below the base rate behind high-yield corporate bonds.

However, not only is the floating-rate base rate now higher in absolute terms, but it is also higher by about 1.5% relative to the historic average (where the 5Y Treasury yield has been 1% above the 3-month yield on average). In other words, while ARDC net income may go lower, it is starting at a very favorable place by historic terms.

Three, although the market expects the Fed to keep cutting the policy rate, the risk is not as asymmetric as it was last year when there was no chance of further hikes. The market consensus is for inflation to rise at least 0.5%. The Fed believes it will be transitory; however, there are a number of risks. Not only is there a risk of an unanchoring of inflation expectations (after 5 straight years of missing the Fed's target), but the coming pickup in inflation may not be as transitory as the Fed believes - after all, its first stab at a "transitory" post-COVID inflation period resulted in a massive series of hikes.

Four, the fund's CLO Equity allocation will see its reinvestment option kick in to deliver additional value to make up for the potential drop in investment income from lower rates, particularly if market volatility remains and loan prices stay below par. Managers will reinvest loan repayments into below-par loans and enjoy the ride-up back to par if things go as planned.

Finally, a key feature of the fund and what truly sets it apart from nearly every other credit CEF is its use of fixed-rate preferreds for about half of its borrowings. Specifically, the fund's liabilities are composed of $99m term fixed-rate preferreds and a $107m floating-rate credit facility.

The preferreds have maturities of 2026-2028 and a weighted-average interest rate of just 2.81%. This is at the same time that nearly all credit CEFs pay north of 5% for their cost of leverage. This is a serious advantage for ARDC and provides it with an additional 1.1% in yield relative to nearly all other credit CEFs.

Takeaways

ARDC remains an attractive credit CEF for income investors. Specifically, it has two key advantages. One, is that its net income is more efficient given it pays less than 3% on half of its liabilities while most credit CEFs pay north of 5%.

Two, its relatively wide credit mandate across bonds, loans and CLOs, creates more opportunity for relative value and alpha opportunities. In particular, its CLO Equity holdings should see their reinvestment options kick in, which will allow managers to reinvest loan repayments into loans trading below par.

The fund's valuation has improved recently as it touched a double-digit discount level (rallying slightly since then). It has once again moved to trade at a wider discount than the Multi-sector CEF sector average.

It also remains an outperformer relative to its sector, as shown below, by a decent margin.

We would look to add to our position if credit spreads move north of 5.5% and/or the discount widens past 15%.

data.title: Is The S&P 500 Mispricing Risk Or Hiding Opportunity? The Case For Holding Steady In A Split Market
data.description:
Right now, the S&P 500 is caught in the middle of two competing forces. On one hand, inflation is cooling—retail inflation just decreased 0.1% in March. That should in theory give the Fed some breathing room. But the market isn’t responding with much confidence. After hitting a high of 6147 in February, the index has slipped nearly 13% and is hovering around 5355. It’s not exactly a crash, but it’s not nothing either.

So, here’s where I land: the market’s in a holding pattern. There’s no strong conviction in either direction and I think investors are just waiting for the next real signal. Maybe it’s the Fed finally cutting rates, maybe it’s earnings surprising to the upside, or maybe it’s something we haven’t even thought of yet. Until then chasing this market feels premature and running from it seems equally short-sighted.

Is the Market Getting This Wrong?

It’s kind of is—but not in a wildly obvious way. Right now, fear is driving more of the narrative than fundamentals. Take the recent bump in gold prices—people are clearly bracing for chaos or at least volatility. But at the same time, consumer demand hasn’t cratered and companies aren’t sounding alarms about a full-on downturn. Inflation data as mentioned, is easing off. Even wholesale inflation decreased 0.04% in March. If the worst fears were actually unfolding, you’d expect the data to reflect that more clearly.

So, while I wouldn’t call the S&P cheap, I do think the sell-off has priced in a more bearish macro backdrop than we’re seeing. That gap between perception and reality could offer a small edge—but again it’s not big enough (yet) to justify pounding the table.

Reading Between the Lines

No single sector drives the S&P, but if you look at the financial sector, which anchors a big chunk of the index, you get some clues. Citigroup and other major players are due to report and the expectations are modest at best. That’s not necessarily a red flag—it’s more a sign that the Street is playing defense. Charles Schwab, for instance, is nudging investors toward “low-volatility high-quality” names, which is investor code for big boring companies with stable earnings. That suggests there’s still money to be made—but you’ve got to be selective and probably a bit patient.

Valuation Check

With the S&P trading around 19 times forward earnings, it’s not cheap, but it’s not crazy either. We’ve hovered around this level before and stuck there longer than people thought we would. Honestly, valuations like this used to raise eyebrows, but the post-COVID market has kind of rewritten the rules. If earnings don’t fall off a cliff—and so far, they’ve held up—the price isn’t outrageous. You’re not walking into a bargain, but you’re not buying at some euphoric peak either. It’s more or less priced where you’d expect in a weird, cautious market like this.

Sentiment Feels Fragile

The vibe in the news lately has tilted cautious. There’s talk of job losses—NY Fed surveys suggest people are genuinely worried about unemployment—and there’s an undercurrent of political risk with tariffs and central bank indecision adding to the noise. Meanwhile, headlines about alternative assets are picking up. First spot Solana ETF? That’s the kind of thing you usually see when traditional markets start to feel stale.

All of this is keeping a lid on investor enthusiasm. People aren’t selling everything, but they’re definitely tightening up.

The Risks You Can’t Ignore

There are plenty. Tariffs are back on the table, which could drag out inflation longer than the Fed’s willing to tolerate. If rate cuts don’t come—or if the Fed decides to keep pushing back because the data doesn’t justify it—valuation multiples could come under pressure. Plus, if the job market actually weakens (not just the perception of it) earnings estimates will have to adjust lower and that could mean more downside.

And we can’t rule out market psychology either. Once people start shifting to “protection mode” it takes a lot to get them leaning back in.

How This Could Play Out

If things go sideways and the Fed holds off on cuts while economic growth slows, the S&P could dip to around 4800. That’s not catastrophic, but it would mean more grinding losses. If the current trend holds—cooling inflation, stable-ish earnings and no major policy shock—we might end up flat-to-mildly higher by year-end. Call it 5600–5800 territory. Best case? Inflation undershoots, the Fed blinks and cuts in Q3, and we catch a melt-up pushing toward 6300. That path exists, but it’s got a lot of hurdles in the way.

That brings me to a more personal question a lot of investors are asking right now—should I just wait this out and come back when things feel safer?

I decided to run the numbers.

Trying to Time the Market? Here's What Happens When You Do

There’s always that voice in the back of your head—maybe I should sit this out and wait for a better entry point. And look, that instinct isn’t irrational. But I wanted to see how much it actually costs when people act on it. So, I ran the numbers.

What I Did

I took a $10,000 investment in the S&P 500, starting way back on January 1, 1980, and let it run through April 14, 2025. No trades, no timing, just a simple buy-and-hold scenario. Then I asked: what happens if you miss just a handful of the best days?

To find out, I looked at daily returns and removed the top-performing days—first the best 10, then the best 20, and so on, all the way to the top 60 days. The idea was simple: how badly does performance suffer if you're out of the market at exactly the wrong time?

The Punchline

If you stayed invested the whole time, your $10,000 would’ve grown to $507,125. Not bad. But skip the best 10 days? You’re down to $220,144. Miss the top 20, and you're looking at $125,877. By the time you’ve missed 60 days, your total return is barely more than $23,000—that’s less than 5% of what you’d have earned by just staying put.

Here’s the kicker: many of those huge up days came right after chaos. Think October 2008 during the financial crisis, or March 2020 when COVID hit hard. Even just a week ago, on April 9, 2025, the S&P snapped back with one of its best single-day returns in years—right after a brutal stretch of selling.

You don’t get a calendar invite before those days happen. They show up unannounced, often when things feel the worst.

So, What Do You Do With This?

This isn’t about being overly optimistic. The market can still slide further. But it’s a reminder that stepping out while you “wait for the dust to settle” often means you’re missing the very bounce that drives long-term gains.

If you're wondering whether to sell now and buy back later—it’s worth asking yourself: are you ready to miss the next April 9, 2025? Because that one day might do more for your portfolio than the next three months of careful planning.

This is why I’m not trying to get too clever with the S&P 500 here. With inflation softening and sentiment already shaky, I’m holding my ground—not because I expect fireworks tomorrow, but because I’ve seen what happens when you try to time the perfect moment and miss.

Final Word

This market isn’t falling apart, but it’s also not offering a clear path higher. It’s more like a slow grind that’s wearing on investor patience. If you’re already long, I don’t see a compelling reason to sell. And if you’re waiting for the perfect entry point—you might be overthinking it.

You can call it cautious, call it neutral—but I’ll call it what it is: Hold.

data.title: DeepSeek Blows Up Meta's AI Strategy
data.description:
Meta (NASDAQ:META) has a huge problem on its hands. What initially seemed like an ingenious plan to corner the open‑source AI market has completely backfired with the rise of Hangzhou DeepSeek Artificial Intelligence Co., Ltd (DEEPSEEK). To make matters worse, a plethora of incredibly performant Chinese models were subsequently released by tech giants like Alibaba (BABA) following DeepSeek’s success. These leading Chinese open‑source models are not only better than Meta’s, but also cheaper. Chinese firms, as it turns out, excel at creating state‑of‑the‑art models even under the hardware restrictions placed on them.

This revelation should not have been surprising in hindsight. After all, a large percentage of AI researchers at U.S. labs like OpenAI and Grok are of Chinese descent. As such, it would not be shocking that there is an order of magnitude more potential AI talent in China given the size of its engineering pool. While top Chinese tech firms have access to an unrivaled talent pool, Meta must recruit from a far smaller one. To make matters worse, the best AI engineers in the U.S. are already heavily drawn to frontier labs like OpenAI, Google DeepMind, and Anthropic.

DeepSeek and the Rise of Chinese Open‑Source AI

The meteoric rise of DeepSeek in early 2025 has completely upended Meta’s grandiose open‑source plans. The Chinese startup DeepSeek completely stunned the Western tech world by releasing the reasoning model DeepSeek-R1, which was nearly as performant as OpenAI’s best model at more than an order of magnitude lower cost and with faster inference. What’s more, DeepSeek claimed it trained its frontier model on less‑advanced chips for only about $6 million. While DeepSeek is likely not being entirely truthful about its access to advanced chips and its absurdly low training figures, it nevertheless delivered genuine breakthroughs in model efficiency.

The massive efficiency gains achieved by the relatively unknown DeepSeek proved that Chinese labs can build state‑of‑the‑art AI systems even under U.S. export controls on high‑end chips. More consequential, however, is that DeepSeek’s breakthrough set off a chain reaction among Chinese tech giants. Just weeks after DeepSeek R1’s launch, Alibaba rushed out Qwen 2.5‑max, a flagship open‑source model that matched Western frontier proprietary models and outperformed any open‑source model Meta had to offer. Likewise, nearly every other major Chinese AI player, from Baidu (BIDU) to Tencent, released powerful models to compete with or surpass the new standard set by DeepSeek.

Chinese tech firms’ ability to corner the open‑source market at frightening speed has rightfully caused Meta to panic. Not only have Chinese open‑source AI efforts one‑upped Meta’s models on performance, but they have also undercut the economics of AI research. Notably, DeepSeek’s earlier model V2 was open‑sourced at a cost of $0.14 per million tokens, roughly two orders of magnitude cheaper than some leading U.S. frontier models charging $15 per million tokens.

In other words, Meta’s strategy of open‑sourcing AI was supposed to give it an edge by harnessing community innovation. Meta would then become the infrastructure on which the open‑source community built their AI applications. Instead, it may have accelerated a race to the bottom in which Chinese firms excel at open‑source AI and drive costs down to near zero. The end result is that Meta has spent countless billions on open‑source models that few will likely use long term.

China’s AI Edge: Talent and State Backing

China’s leapfrog of Meta on the open‑source AI front is rooted in structural advantages beyond Meta’s control. First is the depth of engineering talent in China. In fact, nearly 40 percent of top AI researchers in the U.S. hail from China, reflecting China’s rigorous STEM education system that has produced an enormous pipeline of AI experts. It is often quipped that the real AI competition is U.S. Chinese versus China’s Chinese, and indeed many of the brightest minds driving AI advances at U.S. companies like OpenAI and Google DeepMind have roots in China.

Within China itself, the reservoir of talent is almost certainly far larger. As Meta vies for a relatively small pool of machine‑learning PhDs, especially as other top labs have already drained much of the AI talent in the U.S., China can draw from a far bigger base of engineers and researchers trained at its top universities. When unleashed on ambitious projects like generative AI, this talent pool can achieve world‑class results quickly and with relatively fewer resources.

Second, government support in China will likely only accelerate the country’s AI development. The Chinese state has long identified AI as a national priority and has marshaled vast resources to back it. The success of DeepSeek has further piqued the Chinese government’s interest and drawn billions more dollars in state funding toward AI initiatives and the national push for tech self‑sufficiency. Unlike in the U.S., where open‑source AI is viewed warily by authorities, Beijing actively encourages an open ecosystem to Meta’s detriment.

By promoting open source, Chinese leaders sidestep U.S. sanctions on chips and software by tapping the global developer community to improve their models. This strategy effectively turns the worldwide AI community into collaborators on Chinese‑led models. Even major U.S. tech companies like Microsoft, Perplexity, and Meta itself have built upon DeepSeek’s open‑source models to remain competitive. The U.S. export bans expected to hamper China’s AI efforts have instead motivated Chinese AI labs to focus on efficiency breakthroughs through software and algorithm optimizations. In short, Chinese firms have found ways to do more with less, in stark contrast to Meta, which must rely primarily on its own R&D spending and limited talent pool.

Meta’s Financial Strength vs. Waning AI Leadership

Ironically, Meta’s core business continues to thrive even as its AI leadership in open source is challenged. The company closed out 2024 with impressive financial results. It reported full‑year revenue of $164.5 billion, a 22 percent year‑over‑year increase. Meta’s efficiency drive and focus on profitability also paid off with $62.4 billion in net income, up 59 percent year over year. Meta translated those earnings into $52.1 billion in cash flow, giving it more than enough resources to reinvest in technology. The company’s capital expenditures totaled $39.2 billion last year, among the highest of any tech firm.

However, this large spend may rapidly turn into a negative if Chinese AI labs like DeepSeek continue to outperform Meta’s LLaMA models. The new reality of incredible performance and hyper‑efficient Chinese open‑source models raises doubts about the efficiency and payoff of Meta’s AI spending. Meta finds itself in an arms race where its competitors are not other Western giants, but rivals that can achieve greater open‑source breakthroughs with an order of magnitude less capital and inferior chip technology. The cost advantage and speed of iteration in the Chinese open‑source community threaten to wipe out the benefits Meta expected from open sourcing its own models.

Potential Path Forward

While Meta has invested heavily in AI, the company was never fully on the LLM hype train. Its chief AI scientist, Yann LeCun, is known for skepticism around large language models. Meta has spent much of its resources on alternative architectures like V‑JPEA and LCM. If LeCun is correct that LLMs will eventually hit a wall, Meta’s current predicament is far less worrisome. In that scenario, Meta could be ahead of the competition, given its extensive research into non‑generative approaches.

Still, LLMs appear far from hitting any theoretical wall. The current reinforcement‑learning trajectories of these models seem to follow new exponential growth paths. For instance, OpenAI’s o4‑mini and o3 releases have again blown past expectations. Many argue we are seeing glimpses of artificial general intelligence with these models. Even if LLMs eventually hit a plateau, their capabilities are so vast that losing out on the LLM market could still have a large negative impact on Meta, especially in the near term.

Conclusion

While Meta’s fundamental business is stronger than ever, its path to AI leadership is murky at best. The open‑source revolution Meta hoped to lead is now driven by DeepSeek and Chinese tech giants. This does not spell doom for Meta, as the company still owns some of the world’s most popular social media platforms and generates profits on a scale few can match. Moreover, its war chest and commitment to AI—especially alternative architectures—could yield long‑term results. With that said, Meta is clearly no longer leading the race in open‑source language models, both in quality and efficiency. Given this dynamic, it would be more prudent to downgrade Meta to a hold at its current $1.27 trillion valuation.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Xenon- A Later Stage Story
data.description:
Summary

Xenon (NASDAQ:XENE) is a Canadian biotech company with expertise developing medicines that target sodium and potassium ion channels. The focus of this article is on their lead asset, azetukalner (XENE 1101), which is a Kv7 potassium channel opener being developed for focal onset seizures. The ph IIb data in this indication was positive. The company should report data from two phase III studies by year end 2025. If the trials are successful, they can potentially submit an NDA to FDA. Beyond that, Xenon seeks to develop azetukalner for depression and in primary generalized tonic-clonic seizures. A “pipeline in a molecule” approach is unfolding and this is a key part of the investment thesis.

Focal Epilepsy

1.8 million US and 1.6 million EU patients experience focal onset seizures (FOS). These seizures begin on one side of the brain and are believed to be caused by abnormal electrical activity. Some patients experience an aura which precedes the seizure which can include visual and hearing disturbances. This condition can involve a loss of awareness of surroundings. Motor symptoms that follow can include muscle twitching, spasms and repetitive involuntary movements. This condition, a form of epilepsy, can negatively impact patient’s quality of life. Driving is often legally restricted until a patient is seizure free for a defined time period. Anti-seizure medicines are utilized to control the seizures although they do not cure the underlying condition.

The goal of therapy is to have patients seizure free. The standard of care treatments for focal onset seizures are mostly generic drugs with a variety of mechanisms of action. While there are many generic drugs that physicians can prescribe, Levetiracetam, Lamotrigine, and Lacosamid are commonly prescribed as first line agents. Fatigue, GI symptoms, dizziness, headache, and drowsiness are common side effects. Lamotrigine may have the best tolerability profile but dizziness, blurred vision, headaches and nausea can be troublesome for some patients.

Many patients on anti-seizure medicines need to be titrated to a higher dose to attain maximum efficacy which can take weeks to months. About half of the patients respond to monotherapy. If monotherapy does not prevent seizures, additional medicines are often added. Some trial and error may be required to find tolerable, effective medicines for each patient.

Prescription data suggest many patients are taking multiple medicines. Yet, approximately one third of seizure patients are not seizure free despite multiple medicines. Given this, there is a clear need for new medicines-- especially those with novel mechanisms.

Azetukalner

Azetukalner, is being tested in patients with focal onset seizures. The mechanism of action, a potassium (Kv7) channel opener, has been shown effective. It appears to work by modulating neuronal excitability. Ezogabine, a Kv7 opener was approved by the FDA as an adjunctive treatment for adults with focal seizures and marketed. However, it was later determined that it caused pigmentation of patient’s tissues including the retina. The medicine was withdrawn from the US market in 2017. Azetukalner is a next generation potassium (Kv7) channel opener which thus far has not shown similar tissue pigmentation issues.

Phase IIB data

The phase IIB study tested various doses of azetukalner and compared it to placebo. 285 patients completed the study. Roughly 50% of phase IIb participants were taking three or more anti seizure medicines at enrollment. The median number of failed seizure medicines at enrollment was 6 suggesting this was a refractory, hard to treat population. The primary endpoint of the study was the median percentage change in seizures frequency from baseline. Results revealed a robust dose-response and statistically significant results. For the 10mg, 20mg, 25 mg and placebo cohorts the reduction in seizures were 33.2%, 46.4%, 52.8% and 18.2% respectively. The results were statistically significant for all doses with a p<.001 for the two higher doses. Approximately one third of patients treated for more than 36 months in the OLE experienced ≥12 consecutive months without seizures suggesting sustained benefit.

The most common treatment emergent adverse events were dizziness (24.6%), somnolence (15.6%) and fatigue (10.9%). Two cases of urinary retention were reported. No new safety signals including tissue discoloration have been disclosed thus far in the open label trial which is ongoing.

One important finding was there was a significant reduction in seizures even in patients who had failed more than 6 anti-seizure medicines. Another important finding was that azetukalner showed a rapid onset of action and seizure reduction was evident at one week without the need for titration.

Phase 3

Two phase 3 double blind placebo controlled trials, X-TOLE and XTOLE2, are currently enrolling approximately 360 patients in each trial. Azetukalner will be tested as an adjunctive treatment for adult patients with focal epilepsy. Patients are permitted to take stable doses of other seizure medicines during the trial. For phase III, 15mg and 25 mg will be the doses tested. The number of seizures will be measured during a 9.5 week baseline and the primary endpoint will be the reduction in focal onset seizure frequency at week 12. Topline efficacy and safety data is expected to be released in 2H 2025.

Azetukalner and the Commercial Landscape

Azetukalner is likely to be positioned later in the treatment paradigm and used in treatment resistant patients after less expensive generics have proven ineffective. Some of the benefits are its once daily administration and quick onset of action— Meaningful efficacy was seen at early as at one week. For comparison, Lamictal and Vimpat can take several weeks to reach maximum effect.

In addition, azetukalner does not generally require titration which makes it easier to prescribe than some alternative medicines. (Titration was not part of the protocol in the trials to date.) In comparison, Xcopri, a branded medicine which is very effective and often used in refractory patients, must be cautiously and slowly titrated. There are six doses of Xcopri and titration is done at two week intervals which can make it time consuming for patients to reach an optimal therapeutic dose.

In addition, azetukalner has a unique mechanism of action that differs from other anti-seizure medicines. When multiple medicines are required, prescribers often wish to use different mechanisms.

Beyond that, depression is a common comorbidity observed in patients with focal onset epilepsy. It may be that depression results from disturbances to the brain from seizures or it may be a response to the difficulty managing the condition. Some, although not all clinical trials with ezogabine, (same mechanism as azetukalner) demonstrated improvements in depressive symptoms and anhedonia.

A differentiating factor in this crowded market may be azetukalner’s impact on mood. In the phase three trials of azetukalner, the Patient Global Impression of Change (PGI-C) is a secondary endpoint. An improvement in depression could be reflected on this measure although this is not a direct measurement of depression.

Azetukalner in Depression

Azetukalner showed a positive impact on mood when tested in patients with major depression. A small phase II study, X-NOVA, showed a dose response and an improvement in MADRS which was not statistically significant. However, on HAM-D17, another scale of depression and SHAPS, which measures ability to experience pleasure, a statistically significant improvement was noted. An impact on mood may be an important factor for prescribers. Investors should have a better understanding of azetukalner’s impact on depression when data is released from an investigator sponsored major depressive disorder (MDD) study. Data are expected 1H25.

The X-NOVA2 and 3 trials will test azetukalner 20 mg taken once daily for 6 weeks and compare it to placebo in patients with major depressive disorder (MDD). The primary endpoint will be change from baseline in the Hamilton Depression Rating Scale at week 6. Notably, in the ph 2 X-NOVA trial, there was a statistically significant impact on this depression scale in patients treated with azetukalner.

Investors should also note that depression studies have a high rate of failure relative to other indications. This is especially concerning when it occurs in phase 3 after data previously looked promising. Highly heterogeneous patient populations, complex symptoms, endpoints that may not adequately capture response and high placebo rates are often cited as causes. All that said, phase III trials often are generally powered to detect a treatment effect whereas smaller trials may not be.

Data in MDD is highly important because much of the investment thesis for Xenon centers on the “pipeline in a molecule” approach. MDD is an enormous indication with 21 million patients, many of whom have tolerability issues with existing first line medicines such as SSRIs.

Competiton

Biohaven's (BHVN) BHV-7000 is highly selective for the Kv7.2/7.3 channels. It is being tested in seizures, major depression and recently failed to meet the primary endpoint in a bipolar trial. It differs from azetukalner because it does not activate the GABA receptors. Some effective anti- seizure medicines are known to hit this target. The lack of impact on GABA may explain the absence of meaningful side effects such as dizziness and somnolence. However, the impact on efficacy remains to be established. Investors will need to wait until 2026 to see the results of the studies in focal onset seizures. If selectivity proves to be an asset, the tolerability profile of BHV-7000 may be superior to azetukalner.

Peak Sales Estimates

Azetukalner is likely to be step-edited—insurers will likely require a trial of multiple generic medicines before covering a branded medicine.

Lacosamide, reached sales of $1.5 Bn in 2020. Xcopri, a branded medicine which can be used for refractory seizure patients has been embraced by physicians since its launch in 2019. Last year, sales were approximately $300 million. Topamax, a much older seizure medicine approached $2.9 billion in sales before going generic. These figures suggest there is a sizable market for antiseizure medicines.

Pricing of azetukalner is likely to be similar to Xcopri, a recently launched branded drug (cost approximately $15,000 per year). Given this pricing, significant volume is required to reach meaningful sales.

A Jefferies analyst anticipates that if trials are successful, there may be a $1Bn opportunity in focal onset seizures and an additional $1Bn opportunity in depression. A commercial launch in 2026 in the focal onset seizure indication is possible. The ability to expand into other seizure indications and MDD is critical to support the investment thesis.

Financials

Xenon reported $754.4 million in cash on hand and burned approximately $240 m last year. The company appears adequately funded into 2027 at which time they may have revenue from commercial operations.

Risks

  1. If BHV-700 has a superior side effect profile and a similar efficacy profile, azetukalner may not be a preferred medicine by prescribers.
  2. XENE is a Canadian company and could potentially be subjected to US tariffs as they sell into the US market.
  3. Despite ph IIb success, the phase III trials could fail to meet the primary endpoint. Depression has a lower than average probability of success when compared with other therapeutic areas.
  4. Epilepsy is an indication with many generic drugs and insurers could significantly restrict use. Poor formulary placement due to cost could result in azetukalner becoming a niche medicine without significant sales.
  5. Azetukalner could fail to show an effect in depression and thus the “pipeline in a molecule” thesis may not play out.
  6. Azetukalner could show a latent safety issue such as pigmentation that could result in development or marketing being stopped.
  7. Difficulties with FDA or commercialization is a risk for all biotech companies.

Conclusions

This is a perilous time for biotech investing. There is great uncertainty in terms of how regulatory decisions will be made at the FDA. The XBI and Xenon have traded down in 2025.

Despite the broader backdrop, Xenon may be positioned fairly well. Azetukalner is a later stage asset in an indication outside the more obvious controversial areas. Moreover, it is a medicine which thus far has unequivocally positive efficacy and safety data in focal onset seizures. Given the highly statistically significant phase II results, in my view there is a high probability of ph III trials meeting their primary endpoint. Most importantly, azetukalner has the potential to address a true unmet need with a novel medicine.

A “pipeline in a molecule” approach is unfolding and is a key part of the investment thesis. Investors may wish to wait and see data in major depression to better assess the value of azetukalner.

Neurology and neuropsychiatry are areas where major pharma companies have shown interest. Pfizer acquired Biohaven for $11.6 Bn and currently markets its blockbuster migraine medicine. More recently, BMY acquired Karuna for $14 Bn and launched a novel schizophrenia medicine. J & J kicked off 2025 by acquiring IntraCellular for $14.6 Bn. Caplyta, which can be prescribed for schizophrenia and bipolar-related depression was the headliner and brought in almost $700 m in 2024. Abbvie had a more disappointing outcome with Cerevel.

Lastly, Xenon has a very interesting pre-clinical pipeline which includes a Nav1.7 sodium channel inhibitor. It is believed that NaV 1.7, 1.8, and 1.9 play a role in the regulation of pain. Dampening activity in these sodium channel may translate to pain relief clinically. Developing Nav1.7 inhibitors as an alternative to opiods has been challenging. Xenon is prepared to file an IND for a Nav1.7 sodium channel inhibitor which if proven safe and effective may have significant value.

data.title: Japanese Inflation Accelerates, Complicating BoJ's Rate Decision Amid Global Uncertainty
data.description:
By Min Joo Kang

Fresh food prices eased, yet inflationary pressures broadened in March

Consumer inflation in Japan edged down to 3.6% year on year in March (vs. 3.7% in February and market consensus) as fresh food prices stabilized modestly. Yet core inflation, excluding fresh food, accelerated to 3.2% (vs. 3.0% in February) and core-core inflation, excluding fresh food and energy, also accelerated to a 2.9% pace (vs. 2.6% in February). Both were in line with market consensus.

On a monthly basis, inflation rebounded 0.3% month on month, seasonally adjusted, in March (vs. -0.1% in February) with both goods and services rising 0.2% each. Fresh food prices declined for a second month, yet the earlier pickup in costs is now passing on to other manufactured food prices and services, such as eating out.

April Tokyo CPI inflation data will be released on Friday. Prices are set to accelerate even faster than in March. This will also complicate the BoJ’s rate decision in the near term as trade war risks increase.

BoJ watch

Downside risks to GDP are growing significantly even as inflation is accelerating. Also, Japanese yen trends are being closely watched by the US administration. We dropped our long-held May rate hike call last week, pushing it to July. We expect the BoJ to keep its policy rate unchanged at its May meeting even though inflation is still the key concern. The BoJ is likely to focus on economic uncertainty for now, just like many other central banks. The BoJ will base rate decisions on what happens with concessions made between the US and Japan and where the US tariffs policies go from here. We believe that by July, things will be clearer. Trade concessions will likely take shape by then, giving the BoJ more confidence to raise its policy rate in July. The BoJ's rate hike pace will be quite gradual. For now, we still pencil in two hikes next year - in April and October 2026.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more
